title: Yocto SELinux tool
detail:
Enabling SELinux in Yocto </br>
SELinux Modes and Configuration </br>
Building SELinux Policies </br>
Key Features in Yocto SELinux Integration </br>

title: Enabling SELinux in Yocto
detail:
To integrate SELinux into a Yocto build, follow these steps: </br> </br>
-Add the meta-selinux layer to your bblayers.conf file. </br>
-Update the local.conf file by appending DISTRO_FEATURES_append = " pam selinux". </br>
-Build an SELinux-enabled image using:</br>
    bitbake core-image-selinux </br>

title: Key Features in Yocto SELinux Integration
detail: Kernel Configuration: SELinux requires specific kernel options, such as CONFIG_SECURITY_SELINUX=y and CONFIG_AUDIT=y, which are included in the meta-selinux layer.

title: Startup and Loading Process
detail:
SELinux Startup and Loading Process</br>
1. PRE-KERNEL Phase (Before Kernel Entry) </br></br>

Bootloader (U-Boot/GRUB): When the device powers on, the Bootloader is responsible for loading the Kernel image (vmlinuz) and the initial RAM filesystem (initramfs) into memory. At this stage, SELinux is not yet active. </br> </br>

2. KERNEL SPACE Phase (Kernel Operation) </br>

Kernel Start: The Linux Kernel begins execution and initializes core modules.

LSM Initialization: The Kernel initializes the Linux Security Modules (LSM) framework, which includes SELinux. Internal mechanisms such as the Access Vector Cache (AVC) are established.

The /sys/fs/selinux Interface: The Kernel creates a virtual filesystem at /sys/fs/selinux/. This serves as the "gateway" for Userspace to communicate with the Kernel. However, at this point, the Kernel does not yet contain any security rules.
notes:
3. USERSPACE INIT Phase (Policy Loading) </br></br>
</br>
PID 1 (Init/Systemd): The system init process starts running. It is assigned the init_t label. </br>

selinux-init (Service): One of the first tasks of init is to start the policy loading service.

libselinux & Policy: The libselinux library reads the binary policy file (e.g., policy.XX) from the system configuration directory. This file is the result of compiling .pp, .te, and .fc files, a process previously handled by BitBake during the build stage. </br>

Writing to /sys/fs/selinux/load: The policy loading service opens the special file /sys/fs/selinux/load and "pours" the binary policy data into it.</br>
</br>
4. ENFORCING STATE Phase (Execution)</br>
</br>
Kernel Reception: Once the data is written to the load file, the SELinux LSM within the Kernel loads the rules into memory and switches the system state to Enforcing.

Rule Enforcement: Services like dlt-daemon are assigned labels (e.g., dlt_daemon_t). When they attempt an action, SELinux checks the policy:

ALLOWED: If a rule (from the loaded policy file) permits the action (e.g., writing to /var/log), the behavior is executed.</br>

AVC DENIAL: If the behavior is blocked (e.g., reading /etc/shadow), the action is stopped, and a warning (AVC Denial) is recorded in the audit log file.
